era_context: The High Middle Ages; the Crusader States are engaged in ongoing conflict with Islamic powers in the Levant. The Byzantine Empire under Manuel I Komnenos maintains diplomatic and military engagement with both Crusaders and Islamic powers. In East Asia, the Jin Dynasty controls northern China; the Southern Song Dynasty rules the south. Japan is in the early phase of Taira clan dominance. Islamic civilization is at a high point of cultural and scientific achievement.

events.title: Amalric I of Jerusalem invades Egypt
events.description: King Amalric I of Jerusalem launches a major military invasion of Egypt, seeking to expand Crusader territorial control and access to Egyptian wealth. The campaign involves the siege and capture of several Egyptian cities. However, the invasion ultimately fails to achieve lasting territorial gains; Amalric is forced to withdraw in the face of combined Fatimid and Ayyubid resistance. This campaign is the first of several Crusader invasions of Egypt in the 1160s, all of which ultimately fail to prevent the rise of Saladin.

sources.name: William of Tyre, History of the Crusades

events.certainty_note: Both Crusader and Islamic sources confirm Amalric's invasion of Egypt in 1166. The campaign is dated to 1166 CE.
cross_references: Leads to 1167-002 (continued Crusader campaigns in Egypt)

events.title: Taira no Kiyomori's power consolidates in Japan
events.description: Taira no Kiyomori continues to consolidate his dominance over the imperial court and military affairs in Japan. In 1166, Kiyomori's position is strengthened by the abdication of Emperor Toba, which allows him to exercise greater control over the succession and imperial policy. This period marks the beginning of the Taira clan's dominance over Japanese politics, which will last until their defeat in 1185.
events.certainty_note: Japanese court records confirm Emperor Toba's abdication in 1156 (not 1166); Emperor Takakura ascended in 1168. The dating of Kiyomori's consolidation to 1166 is approximate; his power grew gradually over the 1160s.
cross_references: Part of Taira clan's consolidation of power (1160–1185)

events.title: Shirkuh dies; Saladin assumes command of Ayyubid forces in Egypt
events.description: Shirkuh, the Ayyubid commander in Egypt and Saladin's uncle, dies. Saladin assumes command of the Ayyubid forces in Egypt, a position that will enable him to consolidate power and eventually depose the Fatimid Caliph. Shirkuh's death marks a turning point in Saladin's career; he is now the dominant military figure in Egypt and can pursue his own political ambitions.
key_figures: Saladin (Ṣalāḥ ad-Dīn Yūsuf ibn Ayyūb, assumes command)

sources.name: Baha ad-Din Ibn Shaddad, Life of Saladin

events.certainty_note: Islamic sources confirm Shirkuh's death in 1166 (Rajab 561 AH) and Saladin's assumption of command. The event is well-attested and dated.
cross_references: Part of Saladin's rise to power (1160s–1170s)

events.title: Henry II of England consolidates power in Normandy and Anjou
events.description: King Henry II of England, founder of the Angevin Empire, continues to consolidate his vast territorial holdings in England, Normandy, Anjou, and other French territories. In 1166, Henry holds a major assembly at Chinon to reorganize his feudal holdings and assert his authority over his barons. This period marks the height of Angevin power and Henry's dominance over the English and Norman nobility.
key_figures: Henry II (King of England, Duke of Normandy, Count of Anjou)
events.certainty_note: Henry II's consolidation of power in his French territories is well-attested, but the specific dating of the Chinon assembly to 1166 is approximate. The event occurred sometime in the mid-1160s.

events.title: Averroes (Ibn Rushd) completes his Aristotelian commentaries
events.description: Averroes (Ibn Rushd), the renowned Islamic philosopher, physician, and legal scholar, completes his major commentaries on Aristotle's works. These commentaries, which synthesize Aristotelian philosophy with Islamic theology, become foundational texts in both Islamic and later European intellectual traditions. Averroes' work represents the height of Islamic philosophical achievement in Al-Andalus and demonstrates the continued vitality of Islamic intellectual culture in medieval Spain.

sources.name: Averroes' own writings and biographical sources

events.certainty_note: Averroes completed his major Aristotelian commentaries sometime in the 1160s–1170s. The exact year is uncertain; 1166 is a traditional date but may be off by a few years.
cross_references: Part of broader Islamic Golden Age intellectual flourishing

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Taira no Kiyomori's consolidation to 1166 is imprecise; Emperor Toba abdicated in 1156, not 1166. Kiyomori's power grew gradually over the 1160s, with key events in 1168 and 1169. The dating of Averroes' completion of his commentaries to 1166 is approximate; modern scholarship suggests the work was completed over a longer period, from the 1150s to the 1180s.
historiographic_note: The year 1166 is moderately well-documented in Islamic, European, and Japanese sources. Islamic chronicles provide detailed accounts of Shirkuh's death and Saladin's rise. European sources focus on Crusader military campaigns and Henry II's consolidation of power. Japanese sources are detailed but compiled centuries later. The record is heavily weighted toward military and political events; cultural and intellectual developments are underrepresented except where they coincide with elite patronage. Sub-Saharan Africa and Southeast Asia remain largely absent from surviving written sources.

graph_edges.note: Shirkuh's death in 1166 enabled Saladin's appointment as vizier in 1167
Amalric's invasion of Egypt in 1166 prompted continued campaigns in 1167
